sourceFolder: Vinci/_Archive/Received through 2026-07-08 - Not all processed/Not yet processed/quiz 6

items.notes: Title page: 'Review Quiz #6', Water Survival, SHLSOS Course #549. All five answers clearly legible.
items.extractedText:
1. What should be done if there are a number of liferafts scattered about after their ship sinks?
Student answer: 'Group them up & tie them together'

2. After landing on a beach with your liferaft, what should you do with the liferaft?
Student answer: 'Carry it past the high tide line'

3. What can you do to lessen cold water radiation into the inside of the liferaft in higher latitudes?
Student answer: 'Inflate the floor, close the doors'

4. What procedures can be used if your liferaft is in tropical waters?
Student answer: 'Open the entry ways to the canopy, deflate floor'

5. List the items a lifejacket or personal flotation device should have on it.
Student answer: 'Light, whistle, reflective patches, name of vessel'
keyFacts: Multiple liferafts scattered after a ship sinks should be grouped up and tied together.
After landing a liferaft on a beach, it should be carried past the high tide line.
In higher (colder) latitudes, inflating the floor and closing the doors lessens cold water radiation into a liferaft.
In tropical waters, the canopy entryways should be opened and the floor deflated.
A lifejacket/PFD should have a light, whistle, reflective patches, and the name of the vessel on it.
examAngles: What should survivors do with multiple scattered liferafts? (new)
What should be done with a liferaft after a beach landing? (new)
How should liferaft setup differ between cold/high-latitude and tropical conditions? (a natural compare-and-contrast pairing of questions 3 and 4 -- drafted as one combined question)

What items are required on a PFD? (partially overlaps the already-live q-quiz1a-typeI-pfd-false, which lists 'reflective tape, a whistle, and strobe light' as a true statement about Type I life preservers -- this question adds 'name of vessel' as a new item; drafted with that overlap flagged)
possibleMisconceptions: Leaving liferafts scattered and separate rather than grouping and tying them together for visibility and mutual support.
Leaving a landed liferaft near the waterline, where a rising tide could carry it back out or damage it.
Applying the same setup (floor/doors/canopy) regardless of climate, rather than adapting for heat retention vs. heat/humidity relief.

items.notes: Questions 7, 8, and 9 were left BLANK by the student -- no answers, not guessed, not usable. Questions 6 and 10 are clearly legible.
items.extractedText:
6. Why are only Type I lifejackets required as abandon ship gear?
Student answer: 'flip person over that are faced down'

7. Describe the proper procedures for landing an open lifeboat in heavy surf.
Student answer: [BLANK -- left unanswered]

8. Describe how a survivor would fill the bladder or pillow of an immersion suit with air.
Student answer: [BLANK -- left unanswered]

9. Provided every effort has been made to preserve hydration by avoiding perspiration, how long is it normally possible to survive without water?
Student answer: [BLANK -- left unanswered]

10. What is the safest method of entering the water from a sinking ship if there is an oil fire on the surface?
Student answer: 'Stern, bow, windward side'
keyFacts: Type I lifejackets are required as abandon-ship gear because they flip a face-down person over -- the same face-up/self-righting design principle already tested live for Type I life preservers.
Questions 7, 8, and 9 have no source-supported answer -- not usable.
The safest way to enter the water from a sinking ship with an oil fire on the surface is from the stern or bow, on the windward side.
examAngles: Why is only Type I required as abandon-ship gear? (substantially overlaps the already-live q-quiz1a-typeI-pfd-false's face-up design fact -- not drafted as a new question)
What's the safest entry point/side when there's an oil fire on the water? (new)
possibleMisconceptions: Entering the water on the leeward side, where fire and smoke would be blown toward the survivor rather than away.
items.extractionConfidence: high, except questions 7-9 (left blank, not extracted)

items.notes: All four answers legible. Question 14's answer is terse ('Williamson turn 60°' and '90 = 150°') but internally consistent with the well-established, standard Williamson Turn procedure -- read as: turn toward the side the person fell until the ship's heading has changed 60 degrees from its original course (090°T + 60° = 150°T, matching the '90 = 150' notation). The full Williamson Turn also includes a second phase (reversing rudder to reach the reciprocal heading, 270°T) that the student's answer doesn't mention -- only the first-phase fact is used in drafting, not the unstated second phase.
items.extractedText:
11. Describe the steps taken when trying to swim to a shore where there is a strong current.
Student answer: '45° out of current'

12. What are the steps taken to maneuver your lifeboat to make way while avoiding broaching in heavy seas.
Student answer: 'Keep bow to the seas & use sea anchor if necessary'

13. What is the purpose of collecting condensation in a liferaft?
Student answer: 'Collect it w/ sponge & drink it'

14. Describe the steps the mate on watch will take if your ship is on course 090° T and someone falls overboard, starboard side.
Student answer: 'Williamson turn 60°' / '90 = 150°'
keyFacts: When swimming to shore against a strong current, swim at a 45-degree angle out of (relative to) the current, rather than straight against it.
To maneuver a lifeboat and avoid broaching in heavy seas, keep the bow to the seas and use a sea anchor if necessary.
Condensation in a liferaft is collected (with a sponge) so it can be used as drinking water.

For a man-overboard on a ship heading 090°T with the person falling on the starboard side, the mate on watch executes a Williamson Turn -- turning toward the side the person fell until the heading changes 60 degrees from the original course (090°T + 60° = 150°T).
examAngles: At what angle should a swimmer approach shore against a strong current? (new)
How is broaching avoided when maneuvering a lifeboat in heavy seas? (new)

Why is condensation collected in a liferaft, and how? (combined with question 16 on page 4, which asks the same 'how' with an additional tool -- a t-shirt -- not mentioned here; merged into one question)
What maneuver does a ship execute for a man overboard, and what's the first heading change? (new, high-value -- a well-established, standard maritime procedure)
possibleMisconceptions: Swimming directly against a strong current rather than at an angle, which wastes energy fighting the current head-on.
Turning the stern to the seas rather than the bow, risking broaching.
Turning away from the side the person fell overboard, rather than toward it, during the first phase of a Williamson Turn.
items.extractionConfidence: high, except question 14's answer completeness (see notes -- the marking itself is clear, but the answer only documents the turn's first phase)

items.extractedText:
15. Describe the proper procedure for jumping into the water when abandoning ship.
Student answer: 'Cross your arms, legs. Make sure no debris'

16. How would you collect condensation in a liferaft?
Student answer: 'With a sponge, t-shirt'

17. What does a continuous ringing for no less than 10 seconds of the general alarm bell as well as a continuous blast of the ship's whistle for more than 10 seconds indicate?
Student answer: 'Fire & general emergency'

18. What is the purpose of the air tanks located under the side benches of a lifeboat?
Student answer: 'Provide buoyancy'
keyFacts: When jumping into the water to abandon ship, cross your arms and legs, and make sure there's no debris below.
Condensation in a liferaft can be collected with a sponge or a t-shirt (same fact as question 13, additional tool).
A continuous ringing of the general alarm bell for no less than 10 seconds, plus a continuous blast of the ship's whistle for more than 10 seconds, indicates fire or a general emergency.
The air tanks located under the side benches of a lifeboat provide buoyancy -- already established live via q-quiz1a-side-bench-buoyancy-location and q-ch2-airtanks-vh.
examAngles: What is the proper body position and safety check when jumping into the water abandoning ship? (new, distinct from the already-live 'preferred jump height' fact)

What does the ship's continuous-ringing/continuous-blast (10+ second) alarm signal indicate? (new -- distinct from the already-live 7-short-blasts-plus-1-long boat-stations signal; this is the ship's general/first alarm signal instead)
What is the purpose of side-bench air tanks? (duplicates already-live content -- not drafted)
possibleMisconceptions: Jumping without checking for debris or people already in the water below.
Confusing the continuous-ringing/continuous-blast general alarm with the separate, distinctly-patterned boat-stations signal (seven-plus short blasts, one long) already tested live.

items.extractedText:
19. What can you do to temporarily stop a large leak in the buoyancy tube of your liferaft?
Student answer: 'Sealing clamp'

20. When should the tricing pendants be released?
Student answer: 'When everyone is boarded & seated'

21. What oar command is given to take all the way off a lifeboat moving through the water?
Student answer: 'Hold water all'

22. What is the disadvantage of launching a liferaft on the windward side?
Student answer: 'More wind & stronger seas being blown onto the boat'
keyFacts: A sealing clamp temporarily stops a large leak in a liferaft's buoyancy tube -- already established live via q-quiz1a-sealing-clamps, just asked in reverse (what tool do you use vs. what's the tool for).
Tricing pendants are released when everyone is boarded and seated -- already established live via q-gloss-tricing.
'Hold water all' is the oar command to take all the way off a lifeboat moving through the water.

The disadvantage of launching a liferaft on the windward side is more wind and stronger seas being blown onto the boat -- substantially the same reasoning already given in the misconception text of the already-live q-quiz1a-windward-manual-launch.
examAngles: What oar command stops a lifeboat's way through the water? (new -- distinct from 'Oars', 'Backwater all', 'Way enough', and 'Out oars', all already tested live)
possibleMisconceptions: Confusing 'Hold water all' (stopping the boat's way) with 'Way enough' (finishing the stroke and stowing oars) or 'Backwater all' (reversing) -- three distinct oar commands covering different actions.

duplicateCheckNotes: Checked all 22 questions' facts against the current 250 live questions before drafting. Dropped as exact/near-exact duplicates with no new angle: Q6 (Type I face-up design rationale, duplicates q-quiz1a-typeI-pfd-false), Q18 (side-bench air tanks provide buoyancy, duplicates q-quiz1a-side-bench-buoyancy-location and q-ch2-airtanks-vh), Q19 (sealing clamp stops a buoyancy-tube leak, duplicates q-quiz1a-sealing-clamps), Q20 (tricing pendants released once boarded/seated, duplicates q-gloss-tricing), Q22 (windward liferaft launch disadvantage, substantially the same reasoning already given in q-quiz1a-windward-manual-launch's own misconception text). Questions 7, 8, and 9 were left blank by the student. Two pairs of near-duplicate questions within this same quiz were merged into single questions: Q3+Q4 (liferaft climate adaptation, cold vs. tropical -- a natural compare-and-contrast pairing) and Q13+Q16 (condensation collection, same fact with Q16 adding a second tool).
